---
title: "Research"
---

::: {.panel .panel-light}
## Research Overview

My research is grounded in economic geography, urban and regional economics, and science of science. The core question is how scientific careers, knowledge connections, institutions, and cities are organized across space, and how these relations are associated with urban and regional innovation.

The main empirical setting is elite biomedical scientists' mobility across institutions and cities. Related projects on urban resilience, China-U.S. biomedical innovation systems, and global trade networks extend the same training in relational data, longitudinal comparison, and spatial network analysis.
:::

::: {.panel .panel-muted}
## Core Research Directions

::: {.research-grid}
::: {.research-card}
### Scientific Mobility and Career Trajectories

I examine how scientists move across institutions and cities, how individual career histories can be represented as longitudinal records, and how destination choice can be studied with event-level candidate sets.
:::

::: {.research-card}
### Urban and Regional Innovation

This line connects scientist mobility, institutional platforms, collaboration networks, and city-level opportunities to observed differences in urban and regional innovation systems.
:::

::: {.research-card}
### Scientific Knowledge Networks and Relational Data

I build and audit linked scientist-institution-publication-city data, keeping affiliations, publication locations, collaboration ties, and mobility events analytically distinct.
:::

::: {.research-card}
### Urban Resilience and High-Frequency Spatial Data

Work on daily nighttime lights extends the methodological toolkit to high-frequency spatial data, time-series trajectories, and city-cluster comparison.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.panel .panel-light}
## Data and Methods

::: {.method-list}
- Longitudinal multi-source data construction
- Scientist-institution-city linkage and entity resolution
- Social network analysis and ego-network analysis
- Conditional Logit models with event-level candidate destinations
- ERGM/WERGM specifications for network analysis
- GIS, ArcGIS, Python, R, Stata, NetworkX, Gephi, and Ucinet
:::
:::

::: {.panel .panel-dark}
## Current Research Agenda

::: {.agenda-list}
::: {.agenda-item}
### Scientific Mobility, Knowledge Networks, and Urban Innovation

This flagship programme studies top scientists' career mobility across institutions and cities, and the relations among knowledge-space connections, collaboration networks, research platforms, and urban innovation conditions.
:::

::: {.agenda-item}
### Destination Choice among Elite Biomedical Scientists

The working paper focuses on whether pre-move publication-location exposure is associated with subsequent destination choice among candidate cities in elite biomedical scientist mobility events.
:::

::: {.agenda-item}
### Comparative and Methodological Extensions

China-U.S. biomedical innovation comparison, daily nighttime-light resilience analysis, and earlier global trade network research broaden the empirical domains while retaining the focus on spatial organization, networks, and longitudinal evidence.
:::
:::
:::
